sources.notes: Hard version raises x to 1e12, so simulating every deletion round is impossible.
Official reference code advances k through many identical reverse-deletion steps at once using quotient grouping.

annotation.summary: Find the k-th remaining natural number after x rounds of deleting every y-th remaining element, with x up to 1e12.
annotation.constraints: x,y,k can be 1e12, so the easy binary search with O(x) check is too slow.
annotation.core_idea: Invert the deletion process on the desired rank k. One reverse step maps a surviving rank k to k + floor((k-1)/(y-1)), because every block of y-1 survivors had one deleted number inserted before it. The value of floor((k-1)/(y-1)) stays constant for a range of steps, so group many reverse steps together and add cnt*cur to k. If y=1 or k exceeds 1e12, the answer is -1.
annotation.complexity: O(number of quotient changes), logarithmic-ish in k for practical bounds.

solution_variants.name: grouped-reverse-rank
solution_variants.summary: Repeatedly undo deletion rounds on rank k, batching consecutive rounds where floor((k-1)/(y-1)) is constant.

tags.evidence: The hard solution groups many rounds with the same quotient floor((k-1)/(y-1)) instead of iterating all x rounds.
The reverse process greedily applies the maximum number of currently identical quotient steps before recomputing.
The rank transform is based on floor division by y-1 and multiples removed every y positions.
